steps.title: Find the y-intercept for Function I
steps.content:
For $f(x) = a(3.3)^{x+b}$, set $x = 0$:
$$f(0) = a(3.3)^{0+b} = a(3.3)^b$$

The y-intercept value is $a(3.3)^b$. This requires computing the power $(3.3)^b$. The value itself does not appear explicitly in the original equation as a single constant or coefficient—you must evaluate the exponent to get it.

steps.title: Find the y-intercept for Function II
steps.content: For $g(x) = a(3.3)^x + b$, set $x = 0$: $$g(0) = a(3.3)^0 + b = a(1) + b = a + b$$ The y-intercept value is $a + b$. The constant $b$ appears directly in the equation, but the full y-intercept is the sum of $a$ and $b$. The sum $a + b$ itself is not written as a single constant or coefficient in the equation—it is the sum of two displayed values.

steps.title: Compare what each equation displays
steps.content: A function equation "displays" its y-intercept as a constant or coefficient when that y-intercept value appears explicitly and uncomputed within the equation itself. Function I: The y-intercept $a(3.3)^b$ is not written as a single constant or coefficient. It requires exponentiation. Function II: The y-intercept $a + b$ is not written as a single constant or coefficient either. It is a derived sum, not a term appearing in the equation. Neither equation displays the y-intercept as a constant or coefficient that is already present in its written form. The answer is choice D — D is correct.
